dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for difficulty breathing at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I can't breathe very well, like I'm running out of air.
He's been struggling to breathe all night.
Can you tell me your name and where we are?
My name is James. I'm on my porch in [CITY].
Can you tell me the date today?
James responded appropriately and pointed to his chest.
Do you mind if we take a quick look at your airway and breathing?
Airway is patent. No signs of obstruction noted.
Are you finding it hard to speak?
Yes, I can only take short breaths.
We see some use of your accessory muscles. That's a sign of respiratory distress.
Your breathing is rapid, and we'll monitor for changes in your body's vitals.
Pulse is 74, strong and regular.
Respirations are 30 per minute.
Blood pressure is 147 over 97.
SpO2 is 85%. That's low. We'll need to give you some oxygen.
Let's hook you up to the EKG machine for a heartbeat baseline.
When did this difficulty breathing first start?
I think it started around three in the morning. It just got worse.
Did you take anything at home before this?
He tried his albuterol but it didn't help.
Have you ever been diagnosed with breathing problems?
Yes. I have COPD from years ago and had a couple of heart attacks.
Were you recently sick? Like with the flu?
Yeah, I had the flu about two weeks ago and been coughing up yellow sputum.
Your blood glucose is 122. That is fine.
Do you currently have chest pain or any other type of pain?
No. I don't have chest pain or dizziness.
Your skin looks warm and dry. No redness or rashes noticed.
James, do you mind if we check your neck?
No signs of neck trauma or jugular vein distension noted.
We are going to do a quick EKG to check your heart.
We're going to do a nebulizer treatment now to open up your airways. Can you take a deep breath from this?
Administering 5 mg Albuterol and 0.5 mg Atrovent in the nebulizer. Keep this close to your mouth.
According to the protocol, this is a COPD exacerbation. Considering you've had three STEMIs in the past, we'll be cautious and repeat EKGs to rule out cardiac involvement.
Let's do another quick EKG to make sure there's no cardiac concern.
We'll do a 4-lead ECG to monitor you more closely. Just a few more moments.
The EKG reads sinus rhythm. We'll keep monitoring just in case.
Jim, will you tell us the name of the hospital you prefer?
I prefer the VA Hospital.
We called the VA and were advised to go to [COMPANY_NAME] because it's closer.
Alright, I trust you.
We're going to gently move you onto the stretcher now. Let me know if that's okay.
Okay, just go slow.
Secure and safe for transport. Everyone get comfortable.
We are on our way to [COMPANY_NAME]. I'm going to update them on the phone now.
ED has been informed. They're expecting us soon.
Jim, can we get your identification or insurance card for the record?
I don't have any ID or insurance today.
No problem. We can make a note of that on the PCR and proceed without it.
ABCs are present. Your airway, breathing, and circulation are all stable.
You are alert and oriented (A&Ox4) with a GCS of 15.
You have no significant chest pain reported. Breathing difficulty seems to have improved.
Positive PMS. No signs of decreased mental status.
Doctor, we notice the patient has a defibrillator. No known issues with it at this time.
Let's take one last set of vitals before transfer. Pulse is 90, respirations 26, blood pressure 119 over 78, SpO2 98, and blood glucose is 122 with no chest pain reported.
PCR is ready for documentation and notes transferred. ED nurse has taken care.
Jim is signed over, and we've noted no insurance issues to flag in the records.
ED staff. They're going to assist you up onto the bed now.
On behalf of the crew, best wishes for your recovery. We'll be in touch for the PCR.
No ID on file here, but all the PCR info is here for hospital to view and continue your care.

A 12-lead EKG was obtained, and no STEMI was found. The patient has a history of three STEMIs with the most recent five years ago. Multiple assessments showed sinus rhythm. Equal bilateral chest rise and fall was confirmed during evaluation.
Our PCR is complete, and the ED is aware of your medical history and current stability. He had a documented blood glucose level of 122 during the assessment.
James is still alert, speaking normally, and vitals stable.
No signs of backup issues mid-transit; all procedures have been covered.
Smooth ride in. Monitor cleared. Wake-up procedure confirmed.
All good signs. Exit on protocol medications and transport complete.
